theory.question: Characterize data-intensive applications versus compute-intensive ones.
theory.answer:
"Raw CPU power is rarely a limiting factor for these applications--bigger problems are usually the amount of data, the complexity of data, and the speed at which it is changing."

A data-intensive application is one whose hard part is storing, finding, moving, and processing data correctly as that data grows or changes. A compute-intensive application is mainly limited by CPU cycles, such as video encoding, numerical simulation, or cryptography.

In data-intensive systems, the exam focus is usually on data volume, data complexity, update rate, correctness, and performance under load. The system may need databases for durable storage, indexes for search, caches for fast reads, queues for asynchronous work, and batch or stream processors for derived data.

The important distinction is the bottleneck: adding faster CPUs may not help if the real pressure is disk I/O, network traffic, fan-out, coordination, or maintaining consistency between several data stores. Comment: A payments ledger, activity feed, metrics platform, and search engine can all be data-intensive even if each individual request uses little CPU.

theory.question: List standard building blocks of data-intensive applications.
theory.answer:
Kleppmann says many applications need to "Store data so that they, or another application, can find it again later (databases)," "Remember the result of an expensive operation, to speed up reads (caches)," "Allow users to search data by keyword or filter it in various ways (search indexes)," "Send a message to another process, to be handled asynchronously (stream processing)," and "Periodically crunch a large amount of accumulated data (batch processing)."

These are the standard building blocks because most products need some mix of durable state, fast reads, flexible lookup, asynchronous coordination, and offline computation. In an exam answer, mention that these tools are abstractions: engineers normally do not write a storage engine, cache, or search index from scratch.

The design work is choosing the right tool and combining tools correctly. For example, PostgreSQL may store source-of-truth records, Redis may cache hot objects, Elasticsearch may serve keyword search, Kafka may carry events, and Spark may compute reports. The challenge is that each component has different performance and consistency behavior.

theory.question: Explain why databases, queues, caches, and indexes can be grouped as data systems.
theory.answer:
"There are datastores that are also used as message queues (Redis), and there are message queues with database-like durability guarantees (Apache Kafka)." Kleppmann's point is that the old categories are no longer clean: "The boundaries between the categories are becoming blurred."

We group databases, queues, caches, and indexes as data systems because they all store or move data with particular access patterns, performance characteristics, durability guarantees, and failure modes. Modern applications often cannot satisfy all requirements with one tool, so they combine several: a database for durable truth, a cache for latency, a search index for text queries, and a queue or log for asynchronous processing.

Once an application stitches these tools together and hides them behind an API, it has effectively built a new special-purpose data system. The designer must then provide system-level guarantees, such as keeping cache entries and search indexes in sync with the main database.

Comment: Redis Streams and Kafka logs show why storage and messaging overlap: https://redis.io/docs/latest/develop/data-types/streams/ and https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#intro_concepts_and_terms

theory.question: Define reliability for software systems.
theory.answer:
"Reliability" means, roughly, "continuing to work correctly, even when things go wrong." The phrase "work correctly" includes more than returning responses: the application should perform the expected function, tolerate user mistakes, provide acceptable performance under expected load, and prevent unauthorized access or abuse.

The things that go wrong are faults, and "systems that anticipate faults and can cope with them are called fault-tolerant or resilient." Reliability is not the fantasy of surviving every possible event; it means deciding which faults matter and designing mechanisms that stop those faults from becoming user-visible failures.

Chapter 1 names three major fault classes: hardware faults, software errors, and human errors. A reliable data system might replicate data across machines, restart crashed processes, roll out changes gradually, monitor error rates, and validate invariants at runtime.

For an exam, stress that reliability is a user-facing property of the whole system, not a property of a single perfect component. Comment: Reliability matters even for ordinary apps because corruption, downtime, and incorrect results can hurt users and businesses.

theory.question: Distinguish a fault from a failure.
theory.answer:
"A fault is usually defined as one component of the system deviating from its spec, whereas a failure is when the system as a whole stops providing the required service to the user." This distinction is central to reliability.

Faults are expected in real systems: disks fail, processes crash, bad inputs trigger bugs, dependencies slow down, operators misconfigure services, and networks drop packets. A failure happens only when those internal problems escape containment and affect the service observed by users.

Since "it is impossible to reduce the probability of a fault to zero," the practical goal is fault tolerance: build mechanisms that prevent common faults from causing failures. Examples include replication, retries with limits, failover, process isolation, backups, idempotent jobs, monitoring, and rollback.

Kleppmann also notes that deliberately injecting faults can be useful because it exercises the recovery path; Netflix Chaos Monkey is his example. Comment: One database replica going down is a fault; users losing the ability to read or write their data is a failure.

theory.question: Describe how reliable systems reduce human-error impact.
theory.answer:
Kleppmann emphasizes that humans are part of the system: "Humans design and build software systems, and the operators who keep the systems running are also human." Since configuration errors are a major cause of outages, reliable systems reduce both the chance of human mistakes and the blast radius when mistakes happen.

The book recommends several approaches. First, design abstractions, APIs, and admin interfaces that make the right action easy and the wrong action harder, without being so restrictive that people bypass them. Second, "Decouple the places where people make the most mistakes from the places where they can cause failures," for example by giving engineers sandbox environments with real-looking data but no production impact.

Third, test thoroughly at multiple levels. Fourth, "Allow quick and easy recovery from human errors" through rollback, gradual rollout, and recomputation tools. Finally, provide clear monitoring and telemetry so teams can detect warning signs and diagnose failures.

Comment: Gradual deployment and rollback are standard production safety tools; see Kubernetes rollbacks: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#rolling-back-a-deployment

theory.question: Define scalability and the questions it should answer.
theory.answer:
"Scalability is the term we use to describe a system's ability to cope with increased load." However, Kleppmann warns that "it is meaningless to say 'X is scalable' or 'Y doesn't scale'" as if scalability were a one-dimensional label.

A good answer must name the kind of growth and the performance goal. The two key questions are: "If the system grows in a particular way, what are our options for coping with the growth?" and "How can we add computing resources to handle the additional load?"

For example, a system may grow in concurrent users, requests per second, data volume, write rate, read fan-out, cache misses, or number of tenants. The right response depends on the bottleneck: more replicas may help read-heavy traffic, partitioning may help large datasets, queues may smooth bursts, and precomputation may help expensive reads.

Scalability also requires quantitative performance measures such as response time percentiles or throughput. Comment: Always state both the load parameter and the target, such as p99 under 1 second at 10x read traffic.

theory.question: Describe load parameters.
theory.answer:
"Load can be described with a few numbers which we call load parameters." These parameters are workload-specific measurements that summarize the pressure placed on a system. Kleppmann gives examples such as "requests per second," "the ratio of reads to writes," "the number of simultaneously active users," and "the hit rate on a cache."

The best load parameters depend on the architecture and the real bottleneck. In Twitter's home timeline example, simply counting tweet writes is misleading: the deeper issue is fan-out, because each user follows many people and may be followed by many people. The distribution of followers per user becomes a key load parameter, since one celebrity tweet can generate millions of timeline writes.

For an exam, explain that load parameters let you reason concretely about growth: first describe today's workload, then ask what happens if one parameter doubles or grows by 10x. Good load descriptions also include skew and extreme cases, not only averages, because a small number of unusually heavy users or objects can dominate the design.

theory.question: Distinguish latency from response time.
theory.answer:
Kleppmann notes that latency and response time are often used as synonyms, but they are different. "The response time is what the client sees": the total time between sending a request and receiving a response. It includes actual service time, network delays, and queueing delays.

By contrast, "Latency is the duration that a request is waiting to be handled--during which it is latent, awaiting service." In other words, latency is waiting time, while response time is the end-to-end user-observed duration.

This distinction matters because a request can be slow even if the server's computation is fast: it may wait behind other requests, suffer packet loss and TCP retransmission, pause for garbage collection, hit a disk page fault, or be delayed by a context switch.

In online systems, response time is usually the more important metric because it directly affects users. For an exam, also mention that response time is not a single stable number; it is a distribution, so we should describe it with percentiles rather than only one average.

theory.question: Explain why percentiles are better than mean response time.
theory.answer:
"The mean is not a very good metric if you want to know your 'typical' response time, because it doesn't tell you how many users actually experienced that delay." Response times form a distribution with outliers, so the arithmetic mean can hide the pain of slow requests.

Percentiles are better because they state what fraction of requests are faster than a threshold. The median, or p50, tells you that half of requests are faster and half are slower. Higher percentiles such as p95, p99, and p999 show tail behavior: for example, p95 of 1.5 seconds means 95% of requests are under 1.5 seconds and 5% are at least that slow.

Kleppmann writes that "High percentiles of response times, also known as tail latencies, are important because they directly affect users' experience of the service." They are also useful in SLOs and SLAs, where a service may be considered effectively unavailable if high-percentile response times exceed a target.

Comment: Averages can look healthy while many high-value or data-heavy users are suffering.

theory.question: Define tail latency amplification.
theory.answer:
Tail latency amplification happens when one user-facing request depends on many backend calls. Even if most backend calls are fast, the user request must usually wait for the slowest required call.

Kleppmann states the rule clearly: "It takes just one slow call to make the entire end-user request slow." The risk compounds with fan-out: "The chance of getting a slow call increases if an end-user request requires multiple backend calls."

For example, a page that fetches profile data, recommendations, permissions, notifications, and search results in parallel may have a much worse p99 than any individual service appears to have alone. This is why backend teams care about high percentiles, queueing, timeouts, and dependency budgets.

It also explains why p50 is insufficient: the user may make many requests during a session, and the probability that at least one is slow rises quickly. Comment: Dean and Barroso's "The Tail at Scale" is the classic source on this effect: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2408776.2408794

theory.question: Compare vertical and horizontal scaling.
theory.answer:
Kleppmann describes the common contrast as "scaling up (vertical scaling, moving to a more powerful machine)" versus "scaling out (horizontal scaling, distributing the load across multiple smaller machines)." Vertical scaling keeps the system simpler because there are fewer machines and less distributed coordination, but high-end machines can become expensive and have physical limits.

Horizontal scaling can handle larger workloads and improve availability, but it adds complexity: partitioning, replication, distributed failure modes, network delays, rebalancing, and operational overhead. "Distributing load across multiple machines is also known as a shared-nothing architecture," because each node owns its own CPU, memory, and disks rather than sharing one big machine.

The book's exam-worthy nuance is that this is not a religious choice. Good architectures often use a pragmatic mixture, such as several fairly powerful machines rather than either one giant machine or thousands of tiny instances.

For stateful data systems, Kleppmann notes that distribution is especially complex, so many teams scale a database up until cost, load, or availability requirements force scale-out.

theory.question: Explain why scalable architecture depends on the application.
theory.answer:
"There is no such thing as a generic, one-size-fits-all scalable architecture." A scalable design must match the application's load parameters and assumptions about common and rare operations.

Kleppmann lists possible bottlenecks: "the volume of reads," "the volume of writes," "the volume of data to store," "the complexity of the data," "the response time requirements," and "the access patterns." Two systems with the same data throughput can need completely different designs: the book contrasts 100,000 requests per second of 1 KB each with 3 requests per minute of 2 GB each.

Twitter's timeline example shows the same principle: since home timeline reads greatly outnumber tweet writes, Twitter moved work from read time to write time by precomputing timelines. But that created a new fan-out problem for users with millions of followers, so the final design became hybrid.

For an exam, say that scalable architecture is built around measured workload assumptions; if those assumptions are wrong, the scaling work may be wasted or counterproductive.

theory.question: Define operability, simplicity, and evolvability.
theory.answer:
Kleppmann breaks maintainability into three design principles. "Operability: Make it easy for operations teams to keep the system running smoothly." This includes monitoring, clear runtime visibility, automation support, predictable behavior, good documentation, sane defaults, self-healing where appropriate, and manual control when needed.

"Simplicity: Make it easy for new engineers to understand the system," mainly by removing accidental complexity rather than reducing useful functionality. Complexity appears as tight coupling, tangled dependencies, inconsistent terminology, state-space explosion, special cases, and performance hacks.

"Evolvability: Make it easy for engineers to make changes to the system in the future," adapting it to new use cases as requirements, platforms, regulations, and growth patterns change.

These three qualities matter because most software cost comes after initial development: bug fixes, operations, investigation, migration, feature work, and technical debt. For an exam, connect the three: operability keeps production manageable, simplicity keeps the system understandable, and evolvability keeps future change affordable.

theory.question: Explain accidental complexity and the role of abstraction.
theory.answer:
"Making a system simpler does not necessarily mean reducing its functionality; it can also mean removing accidental complexity." Accidental complexity is complexity that is not inherent in the user's problem but arises from the implementation.

Kleppmann lists symptoms such as state-space explosion, tight coupling, tangled dependencies, inconsistent naming, performance hacks, and special-casing. This matters because complexity slows maintenance and increases bug risk: when engineers cannot reason about a system, hidden assumptions and unintended interactions are easier to miss.

"One of the best tools we have for removing accidental complexity is abstraction." A good abstraction hides implementation detail behind a clean interface and can be reused across many applications. The book gives high-level languages as abstractions over machine code, CPU registers, and syscalls; it also gives SQL as an abstraction over indexes, on-disk and in-memory structures, concurrent requests, and crash recovery.

For an exam, add that abstraction is powerful but difficult: a bad abstraction leaks complexity, while a good one makes the system easier to understand, operate, and evolve.

theory.question: Summarize reliability, scalability, and maintainability.
theory.answer:
The chapter summary gives the core definitions: "Reliability means making systems work correctly, even when faults occur." Those faults may be hardware faults, software errors, or human mistakes, and the goal is to prevent them from becoming user-visible failures.

"Scalability means having strategies for keeping performance good, even when load increases." To discuss it properly, you must first describe load with workload-specific parameters and describe performance quantitatively, such as throughput for batch systems or response-time percentiles for online systems.

"Maintainability... is about making life better for the engineering and operations teams" who keep the system running and adapt it over time. It includes operability, simplicity, and evolvability.

Together, the three ideas define what good data systems are trying to achieve beyond functional requirements. A system must store, retrieve, search, or process data, but it is only production-quality if it keeps doing so under faults, growth, and change. For an exam, frame them as nonfunctional requirements that guide architecture, tool choice, and operational practice.

practice.task: How would you refactor Twitter’s architecture for assembling home timelines from approach 1 (page 11) to approach 2 (page 12)?
practice.description:
Approach 1: posting a tweet inserts it into a global collection.
When a user reads their home timeline, the system looks up everyone they follow, fetches those users' tweets, and merges them sorted by time.

Approach 2: maintain a cache for each user's home timeline.
When someone posts a tweet, the system finds their followers and inserts the tweet into each follower's timeline cache, so reading the timeline is cheap.

solutions.solution:
Keep approach 1 and try to survive by adding database replicas, indexes, and larger machines. On every home timeline read, look up the user's follow graph, fetch recent tweets for every followed account, and merge them by time.

This is bad because it pays the fan-out cost on every read, exactly where the load is highest. The book's Twitter example says home timeline reads were about 300k requests/sec, while tweet posts were much lower, so optimizing only the database does not change the core shape of the problem. It may help temporarily, but every active user repeatedly triggers many reads and merges.

It also handles celebrity and high-follow-count cases poorly: following many active users makes reads expensive, and cache misses or slow queries directly hurt response time. The design is simple, but it does not use the chapter's key scalability idea: choose architecture based on the dominant load parameter.

Create a per-user home timeline cache, like a mailbox of tweets for each recipient. When a user posts a tweet, look up their followers and insert the tweet ID, timestamp, and minimal metadata into each follower's timeline cache. A home timeline read then becomes a cheap lookup of an already-precomputed list, plus hydration of tweet and user details if needed.

This is good because it moves work from read time to write time. In the book's numbers, home timeline reads are far more frequent than tweet posts, so doing extra work when posting is a better tradeoff than recomputing the same timeline on every read. It should reduce response time and database pressure for the common path.

The limitation is write fan-out. A user with many followers can create a burst of cache writes, so the system needs batching, background workers, retry logic, and monitoring for fan-out lag. This solution improves the main bottleneck but is incomplete for extreme follower-count skew.

Migrate gradually to fan-out-on-write with per-user timeline caches. First build the cache and backfill it from existing tweets. Then dual-write new tweets through a fan-out pipeline: publish a tweet event, enqueue follower batches, have workers insert the tweet into each follower's timeline cache, and make each insert idempotent so retries do not duplicate entries. Keep reads on the old path until cache correctness, latency, and lag are proven, then switch reads gradually.

The perfect version also treats fan-out as a load parameter rather than an average. Most users can use approach 2 because their tweets can be delivered to followers quickly. For users with huge follower counts, do not eagerly insert the tweet into millions of caches. Mark them as celebrity or high-fan-out accounts, store their tweets normally, and fetch/merge those tweets at read time for users who follow them.

This hybrid matches the book's final Twitter design: do more work at write time because reads dominate, but avoid pathological write amplification for celebrities. Operationally, monitor queue depth, fan-out lag, cache hit rate, duplicate inserts, failed batches, and timeline freshness.
